agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A logistics platform introducing automated route optimization for drivers at shift start, with dynamic recalculation triggers, customer notification windows, vehicle-class constraints, and configurable stop duration. This spans multiple bounded contexts: Route Planning, Fleet/Driver Management, Delivery Lifecycle, and Customer Notification.",
    "bounded_context": "Multiple contexts identified: (1) Route Planning — owns route calculation, optimization, recalculation triggers, stop time estimates; (2) Fleet Management — owns driver profiles, vehicle classes, zone assignments, shift lifecycle; (3) Delivery Management — owns delivery lifecycle, pending status, failure handling, zone classification; (4) Customer Notification — owns notification rules, delivery proximity windows, customer communication preferences.",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Optimized Route", "definition": "An ordered sequence of delivery stops calculated to minimize total travel time/distance for a driver within their assigned zone, respecting all constraints (vehicle class, delivery priority, time windows)." },
      { "term": "Zone", "definition": "A geographic area used to partition deliveries and assign drivers. A driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift. Deliveries belong to exactly one zone." },
      { "term": "Shift", "definition": "A bounded working period for a driver. Route assignment occurs at shift start. A shift has a defined start and end, and is the lifecycle boundary for route optimization." },
      { "term": "Pending Delivery", "definition": "A delivery that has been created and assigned to a zone but has not yet been completed, failed, or cancelled. Only pending deliveries are eligible for route inclusion." },
      { "term": "Licensed Vehicle Class", "definition": "A classification of the vehicle a driver is authorized to operate (e.g., motorcycle, van, truck, freight). Determines which deliveries the driver is eligible to fulfill based on shipment requirements." },
      { "term": "Shipment Class", "definition": "The vehicle class requirement of a delivery, determined by package dimensions, weight, or cargo type. Must be compatible with the assigned driver's licensed vehicle class." },
      { "term": "Route Recalculation Trigger", "definition": "An event that invalidates the current optimized route and forces a new calculation. Three defined triggers: new delivery added to zone, delivery marked as failed, driver manual deviation." },
      { "term": "Next 3 Stops Window", "definition": "A proximity-based notification threshold. When a delivery enters the next 3 positions in the driver's active route sequence, the associated customer is notified." },
      { "term": "Manual Deviation", "definition": "When a driver departs from the system-suggested route order, detected by completing a stop out of sequence or navigating to a non-next stop." },
      { "term": "Stop Duration Estimate", "definition": "The expected time a driver spends at each delivery stop. Defaults to 4 minutes system-wide, but can be overridden by the operations team at the zone level." },
      { "term": "Failed Delivery", "definition": "A delivery attempt that did not result in successful handoff to the recipient. Triggers route recalculation as the stop is removed or re-queued." },
      { "term": "Operations Team", "definition": "Internal stakeholders with authority to configure zone-level operational parameters such as stop duration overrides." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "A driver MUST NOT be assigned a delivery whose shipment class exceeds their licensed vehicle class capability.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a physical and legal constraint. A motorcycle physically cannot carry freight, and assigning it would violate transport regulations. If this rule is broken, the system state is corrupt — it represents an impossible real-world fulfillment.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery on a driver's route, delivery.shipmentClass must be within driver.licensedVehicleClass.compatibleShipmentClasses."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Route assignment occurs automatically when a driver starts their shift, using all pending deliveries in the driver's assigned zone.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an operational policy — the business could decide to allow manual route creation or delayed assignment. The trigger (shift start) is a business decision, not a physical law.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a driver's shift transitions to 'active', a route is generated containing all pending deliveries in the driver's zone that are compatible with their vehicle class."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when a new delivery is added to the driver's active zone during their shift.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an optimization policy. The business could choose to batch new deliveries or append them without full recalculation. It is a policy choice, not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a delivery with status 'pending' is created in a zone where a driver has an active shift and route, a route recalculation event is emitted and the route is updated to include the new delivery."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when a delivery on the active route is marked as failed.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Policy decision — the system could simply skip the failed stop without recalculating the entire route. Full recalculation is a quality-of-service choice.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a delivery on the active route transitions to 'failed', a route recalculation event is emitted and the resulting route excludes the failed delivery (unless retry policy re-queues it)."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Optimization policy. The system could allow free deviation without recalculating. This is a UX/efficiency decision.",
      "testable_assertion": "When the system detects a driver completed a stop out of the suggested sequence, a route recalculation is triggered using the driver's current position and remaining pending deliveries."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Customers must be notified when their delivery enters the 'next 3 stops' window of the driver's active route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Notification threshold is a business policy — could be changed to 2 or 5 stops. The notification itself is a customer experience decision, not a system integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a route is calculated or recalculated, for each delivery now in positions 1-3 of the route that was not previously in positions 1-3, a customer notification event is emitted."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The default estimated stop duration is 4 minutes per delivery stop.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Configurable operational parameter. Explicitly described as overridable by operations team.",
      "testable_assertion": "When no zone-level override exists, the route optimizer uses 4 minutes as the stop duration for ETA calculations."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The operations team can override the stop duration estimate at the zone level.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Administrative configuration capability. Could be extended to per-stop or per-delivery-type overrides in the future.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a zone has a stop duration override configured, the route optimizer uses the override value instead of the 4-minute default for all stops in that zone."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "A driver's route MUST only contain deliveries within their assigned zone.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Zone boundaries define operational jurisdiction. A route containing out-of-zone deliveries breaks the partitioning model and could cause delivery conflicts with other drivers, double-assignments, and SLA violations. This is a data consistency constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery on a driver's route, delivery.zoneId must equal driver.activeShift.zoneId."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-10",
      "rule": "Only deliveries with status 'pending' are eligible for route inclusion.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Including completed, cancelled, or in-progress deliveries in a route produces a corrupt route state — the driver would be directed to stops that cannot be fulfilled. This is a state integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every delivery included in a newly calculated route must have status 'pending' at the time of route generation."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver starts their shift and there are 5 pending deliveries in their zone, all compatible with their vehicle class",
      "when": "The shift is activated",
      "then": "An optimized route is automatically generated containing all 5 deliveries in an efficient order, and the driver receives the route"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A driver has an active route with 8 remaining stops",
      "when": "A new pending delivery is added to their zone that is compatible with their vehicle class",
      "then": "The route is recalculated to include the new delivery (now 9 stops), optimized from the driver's current position"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A driver has an active route and attempts delivery at stop #3",
      "when": "The delivery is marked as failed",
      "then": "The route is recalculated excluding the failed delivery (or re-including it per retry policy), optimized from the driver's current position"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver has an active route with stops ordered A→B→C→D→E",
      "when": "The driver completes stop C before stop B (manual deviation)",
      "then": "The system detects the deviation and recalculates the route from the driver's current position with remaining stops B, D, E"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A driver has an active route with 10 stops and delivery X is currently at position #5",
      "when": "The driver completes stop #1, causing delivery X to move to position #3 (within the next-3 window)",
      "then": "Customer X receives a notification that their delivery is approaching"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A motorcycle-class driver starts their shift and the zone contains 3 standard parcels and 1 freight shipment",
      "when": "The route is generated",
      "then": "The route contains only the 3 standard parcels; the freight shipment is excluded and remains unassigned"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "The operations team has set the stop duration for Zone A to 6 minutes",
      "when": "A route is calculated for a driver in Zone A with 5 stops",
      "then": "The ETA calculations use 6 minutes per stop (30 min stop time + travel time), not the default 4 minutes"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "A driver has an active route and a new delivery is added to their zone but requires a freight vehicle",
      "when": "The route recalculation is triggered",
      "then": "The new delivery is NOT added to the motorcycle driver's route despite being in their zone"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-9",
      "given": "A route recalculation occurs and delivery Y moves from position #5 to position #2",
      "when": "The recalculated route is applied",
      "then": "Customer Y receives a 'next 3 stops' notification since they were not previously in the window"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-10",
      "given": "A route recalculation occurs and delivery Z was at position #2 but moves to position #6",
      "when": "The recalculated route is applied",
      "then": "Customer Z does NOT receive a duplicate notification, but the system should handle their expectation (potential escalation — see edge cases)"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Driver starts shift but there are zero pending deliveries in their zone", "expected_behavior": "Driver receives an empty route. System should indicate 'no deliveries pending' rather than error. When a delivery is later added to the zone, recalculation trigger fires and assigns it." },
    { "scenario": "All pending deliveries in the zone require a vehicle class the driver cannot fulfill", "expected_behavior": "Driver receives an empty route despite pending deliveries existing. These deliveries remain unassigned. An alert should be raised to operations that the zone has unfulfilable deliveries." },
    { "scenario": "Route recalculation cascades: recalc adds a delivery, which triggers another recalc", "expected_behavior": "The system must debounce or batch recalculation triggers to prevent infinite loops. A recalculation should consume all pending triggers atomically." },
    { "scenario": "Customer receives 'next 3 stops' notification but route recalculation pushes them back beyond position 3", "expected_behavior": "Spec is silent on this. Business must decide: (a) send an updated ETA, (b) send a 'delayed' notification, or (c) do nothing. This is a customer trust issue requiring product decision." },
    { "scenario": "Multiple drivers active in the same zone simultaneously", "expected_behavior": "Spec does not address multi-driver zones. If deliveries are partitioned across drivers, the assignment algorithm must prevent double-assignment. If zones are single-driver, this should be enforced as an invariant." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery is added and immediately cancelled before recalculation completes", "expected_behavior": "Recalculation must use delivery status at computation time. The cancelled delivery must not appear in the route. Race condition must be handled." },
    { "scenario": "Driver deviates from route but to a stop that IS on their route (just out of order)", "expected_behavior": "This is the defined 'manual deviation' case. Recalculate from current position. The completed out-of-order stop should be marked complete and excluded from the new route." },
    { "scenario": "Driver deviates to a location that is NOT on their route at all", "expected_behavior": "System should still recalculate from new position, but this may indicate the driver is taking a break, refueling, or lost. Operations may need visibility into this." },
    { "scenario": "Zone-level stop duration override is set to zero or a negative value", "expected_behavior": "System must validate override values. Minimum stop duration should be enforced (e.g., > 0 minutes). Invalid values should be rejected at configuration time." },
    { "scenario": "A failed delivery has a retry policy — should it re-enter the route?", "expected_behavior": "Spec says 'marked as failed' triggers recalculation but does not define retry semantics. Business must clarify: does a failed delivery become pending again after N minutes, or is it permanently removed from today's route?" },
    { "scenario": "Customer notification sent but delivery subsequently fails at their stop", "expected_behavior": "Customer received a 'your delivery is close' notification followed by a failure. The customer notification context must handle this gracefully — likely a separate 'delivery attempt failed' notification." },
    { "scenario": "What happens to unassigned freight deliveries in a zone with only motorcycle drivers?", "expected_behavior": "These deliveries pile up with no driver capable of fulfilling them. Operations must be alerted. The system should surface 'stranded deliveries' that have no eligible driver in the zone." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Route optimization directly reduces fuel costs, driver overtime, and increases deliveries-per-shift. Dynamic recalculation prevents wasted trips to failed stops. However, excessive recalculation could increase compute costs and confuse drivers, reducing throughput. The 4-minute default stop time affects ETA accuracy, which impacts customer satisfaction and SLA penalties.",
    "operational": "Significant operational improvement: automated route assignment eliminates manual dispatching at shift start. Recalculation triggers keep routes current. However, the operations team gains a new responsibility (zone-level stop time configuration) and needs tooling/training. Multi-driver zone handling is unspecified and could cause dispatch conflicts at scale.",
    "user": "Two user groups affected. Drivers: less cognitive load (no manual route planning), but loss of autonomy may cause resistance; frequent recalculations mid-shift could be disruptive. Customers: 'next 3 stops' notification is a significant CX improvement giving delivery predictability, but false signals (notification then delay) will erode trust faster than no notification at all.",
    "risk": "Primary risks: (1) Vehicle class mismatch is a safety/legal risk if the invariant is violated — freight on a motorcycle is physically dangerous and potentially illegal; (2) Customer notification regression — once customers expect the 'next 3 stops' signal, incorrect notifications damage brand trust disproportionately; (3) Multi-driver zone ambiguity could cause double-deliveries or missed deliveries in production; (4) Recalculation storms during peak hours could degrade system performance.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers", "impact": "Core workflow change — routes are now system-assigned rather than self-planned. Deviation detection adds a layer of monitoring. Adoption risk if drivers feel surveilled or if route quality is poor." },
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Positive: delivery proximity notifications improve experience and reduce 'where is my package' inquiries. Negative: inaccurate notifications or notification-then-delay sequences damage trust." },
      { "group": "Operations Team", "impact": "New configuration responsibility (stop duration per zone). Need visibility into stranded deliveries, recalculation frequency, and route quality metrics. Reduced dispatching burden." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Expect reduced 'where is my delivery' volume due to proactive notifications, but new inquiry type: 'I was told 3 stops away but it never came' when recalculation pushes delivery back." },
      { "group": "Finance / SLA Management", "impact": "Better route optimization should improve SLA adherence. ETA accuracy (dependent on stop duration config) directly affects SLA penalty calculations." },
      { "group": "Compliance / Legal", "impact": "Vehicle class enforcement is a regulatory concern. System must guarantee this invariant for audit purposes. May need logging of all vehicle-class eligibility checks." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 52,
    "reasoning": "The spec is clear on the happy path but leaves several critical operational scenarios ambiguous: multi-driver zones (the biggest gap — is a zone always single-driver?), failed delivery retry semantics, notification regression handling, and what 'optimized' means algorithmically. The vehicle class constraint and recalculation triggers are well-defined. Confidence is held below 55 due to the multi-driver ambiguity and missing retry policy, both of which materially affect implementation correctness.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Vehicle class constraint is clearly an invariant with legal/physical basis",
      "Three recalculation triggers are explicitly defined",
      "Customer notification threshold (next 3 stops) is unambiguous",
      "Stop duration default and override mechanism is clear"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Multi-driver zone behavior — spec assumes single driver per zone but does not state it",
      "Failed delivery retry policy — does a failed delivery re-enter the route?",
      "Notification regression — what happens when a notified customer gets pushed back beyond 3 stops",
      "Definition of 'optimized' — minimize distance? time? prioritize SLA deadlines?",
      "Deviation detection mechanism — GPS-based? sequence-based? What is the detection threshold?"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Each zone has at most one active driver per shift (not stated but implied by 'the driver' singular language)",
      "Route optimization means minimizing total travel time, not distance or other criteria",
      "Deviation is detected by sequence violation (completing a stop out of order), not by GPS tracking off-route",
      "Failed deliveries are removed from the route and not automatically retried",
      "Customer notifications are idempotent — a customer already notified is not re-notified on recalculation unless they left and re-entered the window",
      "Vehicle class compatibility is a strict hierarchy (motorcycle < van < truck < freight)"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec does not address multi-driver zones. It uses singular 'the driver' language, implying one driver per zone, but this is not stated as a constraint. If multiple drivers can be active in the same zone, the delivery assignment algorithm must partition deliveries to prevent double-assignment — a fundamentally different problem than single-driver route optimization.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team", "Customers"],
      "options": [
        "A: Enforce single-driver-per-zone as an invariant — simpler but limits operational flexibility",
        "B: Support multi-driver zones with delivery partitioning — more complex but realistic at scale",
        "C: Allow multi-driver zones but require manual delivery-to-driver assignment before route optimization"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Clarify with product team. Recommend option B for production viability, but implement option A first as MVP since the spec language implies it."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Failed delivery retry semantics are undefined. When a delivery is 'marked as failed', the spec says to recalculate the route, but does not say whether the failed delivery can re-enter the route (e.g., after 30 minutes, retry). This affects route calculation, customer notifications, and SLA tracking.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Drivers", "Operations Team", "SLA Management"],
      "options": [
        "A: Failed deliveries are permanently removed from today's route — simple but may miss successful retry opportunities",
        "B: Failed deliveries re-enter as pending after a configurable cool-down period — more complex but improves delivery success rate",
        "C: Failed deliveries are flagged for manual operations decision — balanced but adds operational burden"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B is standard in the logistics industry. Recommend configurable retry policy per zone with a default of 1 retry after 30 minutes."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The notification regression scenario is not addressed. If a customer receives a 'next 3 stops' notification and then a route recalculation pushes them beyond position 3, the customer has a broken expectation. This is a customer trust issue.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Customer Support"],
      "options": [
        "A: Send an updated ETA notification when delivery is pushed back — transparent but may annoy customers",
        "B: Send a 'slight delay' notification only if pushed back significantly (e.g., beyond position 6) — balanced approach",
        "C: Do nothing — simplest but creates silent expectation violations"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B — notify on significant regression to maintain customer trust without notification fatigue."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "The spec says 'optimized route' but does not define the optimization objective. In logistics, this could mean: minimize total distance, minimize total time, minimize latest delivery time (fairness), maximize SLA compliance, or a weighted combination. The choice fundamentally affects route output.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Customers", "Operations Team"],
      "options": [
        "A: Minimize total travel time (most common)",
        "B: Maximize SLA compliance (prioritize at-risk deliveries)",
        "C: Weighted multi-objective (time + SLA + priority)"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Clarify with product team. Recommend option C with SLA compliance as the primary weight, as this directly ties to business outcomes."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
